turns.text: Hello Angelo, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for asking. Honestly, it’s a lot to try and put into words. Every time I look at that bag by my door, it feels like I’m looking at a deadline I’m not sure I’m ready for. 

Everyone keeps telling me I’m “low risk,” like that’s supposed to make me feel better. They see a healthy nineteen-year-old on their chart, so they check off all the boxes and move on to the next patient. But they don't see the rest of it. They don't know that for me, a hospital isn't just a place where babies are born; it's a place where you're vulnerable, where you're poked and prodded, and where you're told what to do. I’ve lost people before—important people—and that stuff just… it sticks to you. It makes the silence in my apartment so loud sometimes.

I want to be excited. I *am* excited to meet the baby. But I’m mostly just scared that when things get intense—when I’m in pain or feeling like I’m losing control—I’ll be back in those dark places from my childhood instead of here in the delivery room. I just want someone to listen to me, not just my vitals. I need to know I’m going to be treated like a person, not just a "successful" birth story.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I spent so much time on that birth plan. I had this vision of sitting down with one of the midwives and going through it, point by point, like it was a shield I could carry into the room with me. I thought if I wrote down how I wanted to be touched, or what kind of language I needed, it would finally make them *see* me—not just as a nineteen-year-old on her own, but as someone who needed a bit of extra space to stay grounded.

I wanted it to be this guide, a way to keep control when my head started drifting back to those dark places. But when I actually brought it up? It felt like they were just skimming a menu. They were polite, yeah, but it felt like they were checking boxes—"low-risk," "no interventions," "natural birth." They acted like the plan was a luxury, something for people who have it all figured out. 

When labor actually started, that paper felt like it belonged to a different person. Once the contractions really hit, the staff stopped looking at the plan and started looking at the monitors. The "how" of my birth plan vanished in the rush of the hospital routine. They didn't see the person; they saw the process. I didn't get to be heard; I just had to be quiet and get through it.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

It wasn’t one big, dramatic moment where someone ripped the paper out of my hands. It was more like a slow erosion. I had written down that I really needed advance warning before any internal exams—I told them I needed to feel like I had some say over my own body, because that hasn’t always been true for me. 

But when the room got crowded and the monitors started beeping, that went out the window. There was this one moment where I was already reeling from a contraction, and a nurse—who was perfectly nice, really—just reached in without asking or checking in. It was like I wasn't even there. I froze. I wanted to scream, or maybe just go completely still and shut down, which is what I usually do when things get overwhelming.

I felt like I was back in those fluorescent-lit hallways I remember from when I was a kid. The autonomy I’d tried so hard to build with that piece of paper just evaporated. They were focused on the baby’s heart rate, and I get that, I do—the baby is the priority—but in that split second, I wasn't a person anymore. I was just a vessel they were managing. It made me feel small. It made me feel like I’d failed to protect myself, even when I tried to prepare.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I thought the conversation about pain would be a real partnership. I imagined the midwives sitting with me, laying out the pros and cons, and asking me what *I* felt I could handle. I thought they’d explain the medical side but also respect the fact that I’m a person who’s been through trauma, and that maybe pain for me isn’t just physical—it’s emotional, too. I wanted someone to help me figure out how to navigate that without feeling like I was just "weak" for wanting relief or "stubborn" for trying to avoid it.

But the reality? It felt like I was being pushed into a corner. When the contractions got really bad, they didn't really ask me about my options or how I was doing with the intensity. It felt like they were just waiting for me to hit some breaking point where they could offer the epidural like it was the only real solution. It wasn't presented as a choice I was in control of; it felt like a default setting. 

They didn't give me the space to talk about *why* I was afraid of the pain or what I needed to feel safe. They just saw the pain on the monitor, not the fear in my eyes. It felt like they were trying to solve a clinical problem instead of supporting me through a human experience.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I really wanted more than just the binary choice of “tough it out” or “numb everything.” I had read about water therapy—like, just being able to get into a tub—to help take the edge off and keep me grounded. I thought the weightlessness would help me stay in my own body instead of dissociating, which is what happens when I’m scared.

When I asked about it, though, the room went quiet. It wasn't a flat "no," but it was a lot of “well, we’re busy right now” or “we have to keep a closer eye on the monitors.” It felt like there were these invisible hurdles they weren't explaining. Everything was always about the protocol, about what was "standard" for a low-risk birth in that specific unit. They acted like my desire for a more gentle, holistic approach was just a distraction from the real work of delivering the baby.

It made me feel like I was a nuisance for wanting something different. If you aren't choosing the "standard" medical route, you’re suddenly the person who’s making their job harder. So, I just stopped asking. I didn’t have the energy to fight for a bathtub while my body was tearing itself apart. I just took what they gave me, because it was easier than being told "no" again.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I wanted it to feel like a sanctuary. I know that sounds dramatic for a hospital, but I just wanted to walk in and feel like someone understood that I was walking into the scariest day of my life. I hoped for a room that didn't feel so sterile—maybe some dim light, somewhere to put my bag where I didn't feel like a stranger or a patient number. I expected a warm, human welcome, where someone would just look me in the eye and say, "We’ve got you, we’re here for you."

Instead, when I walked in, it was just… loud. The lights were that harsh, buzzing kind that make my skin crawl, and the whole place had that clinical smell that just pulls me right back to the worst parts of my childhood. I felt like a cog in a machine. They were efficient, sure, but it was all check-in forms and ID bracelets. There was no "welcome." It was just “get into this gown, get into this bed, let’s hook you up to the machines.”

I felt like an intruder in my own birth. I was terrified, and instead of safety, I felt exposed. It was like I had to shrink myself down to fit into their system, and the more I tried to make space for my own comfort, the more they reminded me that this was *their* space, not mine.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise, and honestly the one that hit me hardest, was how much the physical space seemed to dictate the care—and how little I mattered in that equation. I walked in expecting that the staff would adapt the environment to help me feel calm, but it was the exact opposite. I had to adapt to the environment, no matter how much it triggered me.

The technology was just everywhere, looming over everything. I expected the machines to be tools, but they felt like the masters of the room. The constant, rhythmic beeping became the only heartbeat that seemed to matter. I remember looking for a corner—just a little space that felt like *me*—but every inch of that room was defined by medical equipment, wires, and charts. 

There was no "soft" place to land. It was like I had walked onto a movie set where the script was already written, and I was just an actor who hadn't been told her lines. I realized in that moment that I wasn't there to be supported; I was there to be processed. That realization made the room feel so much colder than the air conditioning ever could. I felt incredibly alone, even with people standing right there, because none of them were looking at *me*.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I think, more than anything, I wanted respect. Even if things went south—even if the baby needed help—I believed that I was still the one in charge of my own body. I expected that if they needed to do something unexpected, they would stop, look me in the eye, and explain *why*. I wanted them to say, "This is happening, here are the choices, what do you think?" I wanted to feel like a partner, not a passenger.

But when things started moving fast, the conversation just… dissolved. It wasn’t a conversation anymore; it was a series of directives. They didn’t ask for my input; they announced what they were going to do. It was all "we need to do this" or "we’re doing that because of the heart rate." There was no space for me to process, to ask questions, or to hold onto my own agency. It felt like they were doing things *to* me, not *with* me. 

In that moment, I wasn't a person with a history or a voice. I was a clinical event that needed to be managed. When they started making decisions over my head, I felt that old familiar panic—the feeling that my body wasn't mine. It was just another reminder that in their system, I’m only as valuable as my compliance.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

It felt like I was watching my own birth from a distance, like a movie I didn't get to write the script for. "Genuine choice" wasn't even on the table. When you're in the middle of that pain, and people in scrubs are hovering over you with an air of urgency, you don't feel like you’re allowed to say "wait" or "tell me more." If I had asked, I felt like they would have looked at me like I was endangering the baby, and that's the one thing I couldn't risk. 

They used this language that made it sound like they were asking, but it wasn't a question. It was more like, "We’re going to do this now," and it was already happening before I could even find the words to object. It felt like they were steamrolling me, fueled by their own need to get the "correct" outcome on their charts. 

There’s this huge difference between being *informed* and being *consented*. I was informed that they were doing things, but I was never really asked if I agreed to them. I felt powerless. The hardest part wasn't just the medical intervention itself—it was the way they took my agency away, piece by piece, until I was just a body on a bed, waiting for them to finish.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I really wanted someone who would see me—like, *really* see me. I imagined a midwife who would notice when my jaw clenched or when I started to dissociate, someone who would say, "I see you, you're safe, stay here with me." I wanted emotional support that acknowledged that this wasn't just a clinical process, but a massive life event for a nineteen-year-old trying to build a future. I needed to feel like a person who mattered, not just a chart.

But the reality was so distant. The staff was "professional," I guess, but they were robotic. They were focused on the protocol, the cervical checks, and the monitor readings. If I tried to express how scared I was, they’d just give me a rehearsed, clinical reassurance—"You're doing great, everything is normal"—which felt empty because it didn't address the panic I was actually feeling.

It felt like they had a "patient face" they put on, but their eyes were always on the clock or the screen. There was no space for human connection. I felt invisible. I needed a partner, someone to bridge the gap between my trauma and the intensity of birth, but all I got were clinicians who were just waiting to get to the end of the shift. It was lonely, even with a room full of people.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this vision of the "Golden Hour." I read all about it—the immediate skin-to-skin contact, the quiet, the baby being placed on my chest so we could finally breathe together. I wanted that moment to be the point where the fear from the last nine months—and the years before that—finally stopped. I wanted it to be the start of us, just us, undisturbed and peaceful. I thought someone would help me settle in, maybe lower the lights, and just let us be.

But the reality was a blur of chaos. As soon as the baby arrived, it felt like there was this sudden rush of people and activity that had nothing to do with me and my baby being together. They were hovering, talking, and doing things that felt like a whirlwind. My baby wasn't placed on me; they were whisked away to some station for checks I didn't even understand. 

I felt like an observer at my own child’s birth. The silence I’d craved was replaced by the noise of the team, and that peaceful connection I’d built up in my head never actually happened in those first minutes. I was just lying there, still feeling like a body being repaired or assessed, while my baby was across the room. It broke my heart a little, to be honest. I was so ready to hold them, but the room felt like it belonged to everyone but me.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

The thing that stays with me, the one memory that keeps looping in my head, is the sound of the baby crying—but not being able to reach out and pull them to me. Everyone talks about that first cry as if it’s this pure, triumphant moment, but for me, it was just a trigger. I was still laying there, wide open and exposed, and instead of having my baby placed on my skin to ground me, I watched them being carried away to a table across the room.

It felt like the separation I’d been fearing my whole life was happening all over again, right in the delivery room. I wanted so badly to shout, "Bring them here!" but I felt so small, so drained, and so thoroughly reminded that I wasn't the one calling the shots. I just laid there and watched the back of the nurses' heads while they poked and weighed and measured, while my own skin felt like it was starving for the warmth of my child. 

I know they were just doing their job, following the steps, but to me, it felt like an eviction. I had done the work, I had carried the life, and in that first, most important moment, I was sidelined. It’s a hard thing to shake—that feeling of being pushed aside when you finally, *finally* earned the right to hold your future.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I guess I thought physical recovery would be like healing from an injury—you rest, you ice, you follow the instructions, and eventually, the pain just fades. I figured I’d be tired, sure, but I imagined it as a manageable kind of tired. Maybe I’d have help, or at least a few weeks where the world would slow down just enough for me to mend. I really wanted to believe that once I was out of the hospital, I’d finally feel like my body belonged to me again.

The reality? It was so much heavier than that. It wasn’t just physical—it was like my body felt like a foreign object. I was exhausted in a way that sleep didn't fix. The physical pain was sharp and constant for a while, but the real weight was how quickly I had to jump back into "real life." I didn’t have the luxury of just focusing on myself. I’m nineteen, I’m on my own, and the bills don’t stop just because I’m healing.

It feels like a blur of trying to be a mom while my body feels like it's falling apart and my head is still stuck back in that room. The expectation was that I’d bounce back, but I just feel… different. I don't know how to explain it. I’m just trying to keep my head above water, and sometimes I feel like I'm sinking.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

No one ever warned me about the feeling of being haunted by my own body. They talked a lot about the physical healing—the stitches, the fatigue, the soreness—but they didn't talk about how it feels to look in the mirror after you've been through that, and see someone you don't recognize. 

It’s this visceral, crawling feeling. My body was the place where I was the most vulnerable, the place where my boundaries were crossed and where I felt the least like "me." Now, I’m trying to nurse, I’m trying to heal, and I feel like I’m constantly being re-traumatized by the physical demands of it. I’ll be sitting there, trying to feed the baby, and suddenly my heart starts racing because my skin feels *too* sensitive, or because I’m touched out, or because the way I have to sit reminds me of being in that hospital bed.

They give you these pamphlets on "self-care" and "healing," but those sheets don’t account for the fact that my body feels like a crime scene. I wasn't prepared for how much the trauma would manifest physically—the way my nervous system just refuses to settle down, no matter how much time passes. I thought I’d be "healed" by now, but I’m just navigating a different kind of pain.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I really wanted it to be this quiet, sweet connection—the kind you see in photos, where it’s just the two of us finally settling into a rhythm. I thought breastfeeding would be the one thing that was just *ours*, a way for me to finally be the protector, to be the one who could soothe and nurture, especially after everything that happened during the birth. I pictured us lying in bed, the baby quiet and peaceful, and me feeling like a real, capable mom. 

The reality was just… hard. It wasn’t peaceful; it was demanding. My body felt so depleted, and there were times when feeding felt like a constant assault on my senses. I was trying so hard to get it right, but I was exhausted, stressed about work, and worried about money, and that just made everything tense. The baby could feel it, too—they could feel when I was anxious, and that just fed into this loop of panic.

When I struggled, I didn't feel supported; I felt judged. It felt like I was being watched to see if I was doing it "the right way." I didn't get a soft place to land; I got instructions. It made me feel like I was failing at the one thing I was supposed to be a natural at. It’s hard to feel like a mother when you’re just trying to survive the next hour.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I had this naive hope that once the baby was born, I’d finally hit some kind of "reset" button. I told myself that the baby would be the beginning of something whole, and that the sheer joy of motherhood would just wash away all that history I’ve been dragging behind me. I expected to feel like a new person—someone stronger, someone with a clear purpose. I thought the quiet moments would be peaceful, not heavy.

The reality? It’s like the birth cracked something open that hasn’t healed yet. Instead of feeling whole, I feel fragmented. I go from moments of total, overwhelming love for my baby to these dark, hollow places where I feel like I’m nineteen again, trapped in a house I can't leave. The nights are the hardest. When it’s dark and quiet, my brain goes right back to that hospital room, back to the loss, back to the things I’ve survived. 

I’m struggling to find “me” in all of this. I’m either a mom, or I’m a student of my own survival, but I’m rarely both at the same time. I don’t feel like the happy mother in the pamphlets; I feel like someone trying to hold up a crumbling wall so my baby doesn't see it fall. It’s heavy, and sometimes I’m just so, so tired of being strong.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

Oh, there’s a massive gap. It’s like there’s this unspoken script for how a new mom is *supposed* to act, especially one as young as I am. Everyone expects me to be beaming with this "mother’s love," and whenever I *do* feel that, it’s real—it’s the only thing that makes me feel like I’m breathing. But when I feel the darkness, or when I feel like I’m drowning in my own history, I have to hide it.

If I tell a nurse or a doctor that I feel like I’m losing myself, they get that worried, clinical look. They start talking about “postpartum depression screenings” and "resources" like I’m a problem to be solved. I don’t want to be a problem. I don't want them to look at me and think I’m an "unfit" mother just because my past is catching up to me. So, I nod, I smile, I say I’m “adjusting,” and I keep the fear inside. 

Even with family, it’s the same. They want the "perfect" story. They don't want to hear about how the ghost of my childhood is sitting in the corner while I feed the baby. I’ve learned that being "allowed" to feel means being quiet about the hard stuff. It’s a lonely place to be, carrying all that weight while everyone else thinks everything is fine.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I think I expected that becoming a mother would finally give me a definitive label—something that felt solid and permanent, like “Angelo, the Mother.” I thought it would overwrite everything else, burying the girl who lived through all those losses, and replacing her with someone strong, calm, and sure of herself. I thought the baby would be the anchor that kept me from drifting into that dark, lonely place I’ve been living in since I was a kid.

But the reality is so much more complicated. I feel like I’m a mosaic made of broken glass. My identity hasn’t shifted into something new; it’s just expanded into a bunch of conflicting pieces. Some days, I look at the baby and I feel this fierce, protective power—the strongest I’ve ever felt. But then, an hour later, I’m back in the apartment, and the weight of being nineteen and alone hits me, and I’m just that terrified, helpless girl again.

I haven't "arrived" anywhere. I’m still standing at that edge of the cliff I talked about, only now I’m holding someone else’s hand. I’m not sure who I am, really. I’m just trying to keep the girl I used to be and the mother I want to be from tearing each other apart. It’s not the neat transition I was promised.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

Honestly, the biggest thing I lost wasn't a job or a hobby; it was the ability to just *be* nineteen. Before the baby, even with the trauma, I could at least pretend I was just a regular person living a regular life. I could go to work, walk down the street, and just blend in. I felt like I had a future I was building, a version of myself that wasn't defined by what I had lost or where I came from.

Now, that version of me feels like a ghost. When I’m with people, they see the baby, or they see the "young, single mom" label, and they project everything onto me. They look at me with pity, or judgment, or this weird, forced pride, and I can't find that space where I’m just *Angelo* anymore. I feel like my personal identity has been swallowed up by the role of "mother," and it’s exhausting. 

I miss the anonymity. I miss being able to wake up and decide who I want to be for the day, without the constant, heavy presence of a little life that depends on me for every single thing. I love my baby, more than I thought was possible, but I also feel like I’ve had to bury parts of myself just to keep us both fed and safe. And it hurts. It really, really hurts to feel like I’m disappearing.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected these appointments to be my chance to catch my breath. I honestly thought that once the baby was here, the clinic would see the "postpartum Angelo" as someone who needed extra care, especially after everything I’d been through during the birth. I pictured us talking about how I was holding up—emotionally, not just physically. I thought they’d check on *me* the way they checked on the baby, maybe even asking how the isolation was hitting me or if I needed someone to just listen for a second.

Instead, the follow-up felt like a conveyor belt. The appointments were short, focused entirely on the baby’s weight, their growth, and maybe a quick, "Are you okay? No PPD symptoms?" check-box. It was like they were looking at a spreadsheet, not a person. 

I’d be sitting there, trying to figure out how to articulate that I felt like I was unraveling, and before I could even find the words, they were already moving on to the next patient. There was no space for the "whole" person. I felt like I was being treated as a secondary attachment to the baby. They never asked how I was sleeping, or if the memories were still haunting me. It felt like I’d been cleared for takeoff, even when I was still very much grounded and struggling to fly.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I remember trying to tell my midwife, just once, that I felt like I was losing my grip. I said something like, "I keep having these flashbacks to the hospital room when I’m nursing, and I feel like I’m going to jump out of my own skin." I didn’t use big, heavy words like "trauma," but I was trying to reach out. I wanted her to stop, to look at me, and to maybe just validate that what I was feeling was real.

She just gave me this thin, tight smile and said, "Oh, honey, that’s just the hormones and the sleep deprivation. You're doing a great job, just give it time." It was like she’d already decided I was just a tired new mom, and there was no room in her schedule to consider that I might be someone who had been deeply hurt. 

She didn't dig deeper. She didn't ask what the flashbacks were like or how often they happened. She just checked a box that said I was "adjusted," and that was the end of it. It made me feel like I was crazy for even thinking something was wrong. After that, I didn't try to bring it up again. If the people who are supposed to be experts tell you it's normal, you stop trusting your own instincts—you just learn to bury the panic deeper.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I thought going back to work would be my lifeline. I imagined that being around other people, focusing on tasks that had nothing to do with diapers or feeding, would help me feel like "me" again. I really believed that work would be the one place where I could be Angelo, not just a nineteen-year-old mom with a history she can't shake. I thought it would give me a sense of control and independence that I felt like I lost the second I walked into that hospital room.

The reality? It was jarring. The moment I walked through those doors, I felt like I was living a double life. Every single second, I was wondering about the baby, but I couldn't say it out loud because I needed to prove I was just as capable as anyone else. I felt this intense pressure to hide the fact that I was exhausted, that my body was still aching, and that my mind was constantly being pulled back to that delivery room. 

It was isolating. I was surrounded by people, but I felt like I was wearing a mask. Every time I had to step away to pump or handle something for the baby, I felt that familiar, heavy weight of judgment. I wasn't just working; I was constantly fighting to stay present. It wasn't the relief I expected; it was just another stage where I had to perform.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

The biggest challenge was that the world outside—my job, the people I work with—doesn't really account for the fact that I’m still internally shaking from everything that happened. Everything feels like a balancing act where one slip could make the whole thing collapse. 

Managing the physical side of things, like pumping in a closet-sized break room that doesn't feel sanitary or safe, just reminded me of the lack of privacy in the hospital. It’s like, even in my “professional” life, I’m being forced to expose parts of my body or my routine that feel vulnerable, and it constantly drags me back to those memories of being poked and prodded. And childcare? It’s a nightmare. I’m constantly wondering if my baby is being held the way I want them to be, or if they’re feeling the same kind of cold, clinical detachment that I felt when I was in the hospital. 

I’m expected to show up at 9:00 AM, sharp and focused, but my brain is still processing the trauma of birth. I'm exhausted, I'm jumpy, and I’m constantly worried about money. It’s like trying to run a marathon in a storm while holding a newborn. Nobody tells you how heavy that actually feels. I just want a minute where I don't feel like I'm failing someone.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

Actually, I’m doing this alone. I’m not married, and I don’t have a partner in the picture—it’s just me and the baby in this apartment. When I was pregnant, I think I built up this fantasy of having someone there to tag out with, someone to trade off the midnight feeds so I could finally get an hour of unbroken sleep, or just someone to look at me and say, "You're doing enough." 

But the reality is just a relentless, solitary grind. There’s no "we" to lean on. Every diaper, every cry at 3:00 AM, every decision about whether to buy diapers or save for rent—it all lands on my shoulders. I don’t have anyone to talk to about the flashbacks or the way the birth felt, so I just hold it all in until I feel like I’m going to burst. 

It’s an incredible, lonely kind of love. I look at my baby and I’m so proud that I’ve kept us both going, but I’m also so tired of being the only one holding the line. There’s no one to remind me that I’m human when I start feeling like I’m breaking. It’s just me, trying to build a whole life out of pieces I’m still trying to pick up.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise wasn't really a "change," because there was never a "we" to begin with—it was the profound, crushing realization of just how invisible that labor is to everyone else. I think I expected that, somehow, the world would see I was doing the job of two people and would step in to fill that gap. I thought society would be built in a way that didn’t make it *extra* hard to be a single mom at nineteen.

But there’s no safety net. When I’m at the clinic or the grocery store, people see me and they don’t see a parent who is carrying the weight of two roles; they see a "young mom" who they assume has someone waiting in the wings, or someone they can just judge for needing a little extra grace. 

I’m surprised by how much strength I’ve had to dig up from places I didn't know existed. But it’s a jagged, angry kind of strength. I’m surprised that I don't feel "empowered"—I just feel like I’m surviving. I think the hardest part is realizing that the expectation of having a partner was just another piece of the "perfect" picture I was sold, and the reality is that I’m the only one who’s ever going to fully understand what it took to get here.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected that the medical system would be a partner in my transition to motherhood. I thought that by showing up, being honest about being young and carrying a past, I would be wrapped in a kind of care that was gentle and protective. I truly believed the "care" in maternity care meant looking after my spirit as much as my vitals. I expected to feel like an equal, someone whose voice carried weight in her own birth story.

The most significant gap, the one that still stings, is that I never once felt like a whole human being in their eyes. I was always a "low-risk patient," a "successful case," or a "young mother." They treated the biological event of birth, but they ignored the woman living it. They were so focused on getting a healthy baby out that they didn't notice the mother being left behind in the process.

I walked into that journey hoping to finally heal, to close the book on the trauma of my childhood, but the system just added new pages of hurt. They gave me medical safety, maybe, but they didn’t give me emotional safety. I’m left wondering why it’s so hard to be seen as a person in a place that’s supposed to be about the most human experience of all.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I would fundamentally shift the focus from the baby as the only "outcome" that matters to the woman as a whole person whose life is continuing long after they leave the hospital. The system right now is built like a factory—it’s designed for efficiency, for clearing beds, and for hitting clinical benchmarks. It’s not built for the human in the middle of it.

If I could change one thing, I’d want trauma-informed care to be the absolute standard, not a niche "add-on." That means everyone—from the front desk staff to the surgeons—is trained to understand that every person walking through those doors brings their entire history with them. It means slowing down. It means real, honest informed consent where "no" or "wait" is a valid, respected answer, not a barrier to productivity.

I want a system where I don’t have to fight to be seen. I want them to look at a nineteen-year-old and not just see a “low-risk birth” but a person who might be terrified and needs someone to hold space for her. The system should be built to hold the person, not just the pregnancy. If they did that—if they actually listened and saw me—I think the birth would have been the beginning of the healing I was so desperate for.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add is just how invisible the *after* really is. Everyone focuses so much energy on the birth itself—on the date, the room, the labor—but there’s this massive, quiet chasm that happens the second you walk out those hospital doors with your baby. You’re expected to just be a mom, to know what you’re doing, to handle the physical recovery, the loneliness, the lack of money, all while you’re still reeling from the shock of the birth.

There’s no "post-birth" support that actually reaches into your life. You’re left in this apartment, in the silence, with your own ghosts and a tiny baby who needs everything from you. It feels like the system just abandons you the moment you’re technically "discharged." If they want to help, they need to stop thinking the work ends when the cord is cut. They need to find a way to stay connected, to check in on the *person*, not just the baby’s weight. Because that’s when you really need to be held—when you’re finally alone in the quiet, trying to figure out how to be a mother when you’re still trying to heal yourself. It’s a hard, lonely climb, and I just wish I hadn't felt like I had to do it entirely by myself.
